name: The Presidency under Andrew Jackson
constraints: Constitutional limits on executive authority
Congressional control over appropriations and legislation
Dependence on state governments for policy implementation
Supreme Court's power of judicial review
factional_pressures: Kitchen Cabinet advisors pushing for aggressive federal action
Democratic Party leaders seeking political moderation
Southern allies demanding protection of slavery and states' rights
Western supporters calling for Indian removal and territorial expansion
public_legitimacy: High among common white men who see Jackson as their champion; deeply contested among educated elites and Southern planters.
plausible_actions: Issue proclamations against nullification
Request congressional authorization for military force
Mobilize federal troops to Charleston Harbor
Negotiate tariff compromises through surrogates
Remove federal deposits from the Bank of the United States
implausible_actions: Suspend the Constitution during the crisis
Invade South Carolina without congressional approval

name: South Carolina Legislature
constraints: Federal supremacy clause in Constitution
Threat of federal military intervention
Economic dependence on interstate commerce
Limited military resources compared to federal government
factional_pressures: Nullifiers led by Calhoun demanding immediate resistance
Unionists opposing nullification as treasonous
Planter elite fearing economic ruin from tariffs
Charleston merchants worried about trade disruption
public_legitimacy: Strong among white South Carolinians who view it as defender of state sovereignty; seen as rebellious by most other Americans.
plausible_actions: Pass nullification ordinances voiding federal tariffs
Authorize state military preparations
Call a state convention to consider secession
Negotiate with other Southern states for joint resistance
Establish state courts to enforce nullification
implausible_actions: Declare war on the federal government
Form military alliance with foreign power

name: United States Senate
constraints: Equal state representation regardless of population
Requirement for two-thirds majority to override presidential vetoes
Dependence on House of Representatives for revenue bills
Public pressure from expanding democratic electorate
factional_pressures: Southern senators defending slavery and states' rights
Northern senators promoting protective tariffs and internal improvements
Jackson Democrats seeking to support presidential authority
Anti-Jackson coalition forming around opposition to executive power
public_legitimacy: Respected as deliberative body representing state interests; increasingly criticized as aristocratic institution by democratic reformers.
plausible_actions: Pass Force Bill authorizing military action against South Carolina
Negotiate compromise tariff legislation
Censure President Jackson for exceeding constitutional authority
Reject presidential appointments to federal positions
Investigate executive branch conduct through committee hearings
implausible_actions: Expel entire state delegations for disloyalty

name: Democratic Party
constraints: Sectional divisions between Northern and Southern wings
Lack of permanent organizational structure
Dependence on newspaper editors and local politicians
Constitutional prohibition on national political parties
factional_pressures: Southern Democrats demanding protection of slavery and states' rights
Northern Democrats supporting federal authority and popular democracy
Van Buren faction seeking party unity and electoral success
Calhoun faction threatening defection over nullification
public_legitimacy: High among common white men who identify with Jacksonian democracy; viewed with suspicion by established elites.
plausible_actions: Organize mass rallies supporting Jackson's position
Coordinate newspaper campaigns across multiple states
Discipline party members who oppose administration policy
Recruit candidates for congressional and state elections
Establish party machinery in new territories
implausible_actions: Impose binding party discipline on elected officials

name: Second Bank of the United States
constraints: Twenty-year charter expires in 1836
Congressional control over charter renewal
Public hostility toward concentrated financial power
Presidential opposition to recharter
factional_pressures: Nicholas Biddle and bank directors seeking charter renewal
State banks competing for federal deposits
Eastern merchants dependent on bank's credit facilities
Western farmers and Southern planters opposing bank's policies
public_legitimacy: Low among common people who view it as corrupt monopoly; supported by commercial and financial elites.
plausible_actions: Call in loans to create financial pressure
Lobby Congress for early charter renewal
Coordinate with opposition politicians against Jackson
Manipulate credit markets to influence elections
Publish pamphlets defending bank's constitutional role
implausible_actions: Refuse to honor federal government deposits

name: Supreme Court of the United States
constraints: Lack of enforcement power without executive cooperation
Dependence on lower courts for case development
Public skepticism toward judicial supremacy
Limited jurisdiction over state court decisions
factional_pressures: Chief Justice Marshall defending federal supremacy and judicial review
States' rights advocates challenging federal court authority
Jackson administration questioning court's political role
Legal profession divided over constitutional interpretation
public_legitimacy: Moderate among educated classes who respect legal expertise; lower among democratic masses who prefer elected authority.
plausible_actions: Rule on constitutionality of nullification ordinances
Interpret federal supremacy clause in relevant cases
Issue writs of mandamus against state officials
Decline jurisdiction in politically sensitive cases
Uphold federal authority over interstate commerce
implausible_actions: Order military enforcement of judicial decisions
Remove state officials from office directly

name: Charleston Mercury and Partisan Press
constraints: Dependence on partisan financial support
Limited circulation outside major cities
Postal system controlled by federal government
Libel laws restricting inflammatory content
factional_pressures: Nullification editors promoting resistance to federal authority
Union editors supporting compromise and federal supremacy
Political parties seeking favorable coverage
Advertisers and subscribers influencing editorial positions
public_legitimacy: High influence among literate political classes; growing reach among expanding electorate through partisan networks.
plausible_actions: Publish inflammatory editorials supporting nullification
Coordinate messaging across multiple partisan newspapers
Organize public meetings through editorial campaigns
Attack political opponents through personal scandals
Distribute pamphlets and broadsides during crises
implausible_actions: Maintain strict editorial neutrality on partisan issues

name: South Carolina Military Organizations
constraints: Constitutional prohibition on state armies without congressional consent
Limited weapons and training compared to federal forces
Dependence on volunteer militia rather than professional soldiers
Federal control over military supplies and arsenals
factional_pressures: Nullification supporters demanding armed resistance preparation
Unionist officers refusing to participate in rebellion
Federal officers torn between state and national loyalty
Charleston elite financing military preparations
public_legitimacy: Strong among nullification supporters who see armed resistance as legitimate; viewed as treasonous by federal authorities.
plausible_actions: Organize and drill volunteer militia companies
Seize federal arsenals and fortifications
Coordinate with other Southern state militias
Establish military courts to enforce nullification
Prepare defensive positions around Charleston
implausible_actions: Launch offensive military campaign against federal forces
